findings.title: Entirely different revert algorithm — spurious global lookup and merge
findings.message: Go never looks up the global file entry; it simply marks every qualifying local file as deleted with a zeroed version vector, relying on a subsequent pull to restore the correct global state. Rust introduces an unrelated algorithm that queries the global file (line 2044), conditionally merges it into the update set (lines 2050-2055), and only marks the local as deleted when the global also has the receive-only flag. This changes which records are persisted and what their content is, breaking the "delete-then-pull" revert contract.

findings.title: FlagLocalReceiveOnly incorrectly cleared
findings.message: Go explicitly preserves FlagLocalReceiveOnly on deleted items (lines 82-84 comment: "Purposely not removing FlagLocalReceiveOnly as the deleted item should still not be sent in index updates"). Rust does the opposite at line 2043, unconditionally clearing the flag via `local.local_flags &= !db::FLAG_LOCAL_RECEIVE_ONLY`. This inverts the intended index-update suppression behavior and will cause reverted items to be incorrectly advertised to peers.

findings.title: No filesystem deletion of unexpected items
findings.message: Go removes non-directory files from the filesystem via `inWritableDir(f.mtimefs.Remove, fi.Name)` (Go line 72) before marking them deleted in the database. Rust performs no filesystem I/O at all, so reverted files remain on disk even though the database considers them deleted, causing persistent inconsistency between disk and DB state.

findings.title: Version vector not zeroed
findings.message: Go explicitly sets `fi.Version = protocol.Vector{}` (Go line 80) so that the subsequent pull will treat the file as needing the global version. Rust never zeroes the version vector, so the puller may consider the local version sufficient and skip re-downloading the correct content.

findings.title: Missing error propagation
findings.message: The Go Flush returns error and propagates failures from both flushToRemove and updateBatch.Flush; the Rust signature returns (Vec<db::FileInfo>, Vec<String>) with no Result wrapper, so all error paths from both sub-operations are silently dropped. This eliminates the short-circuit behavior where a flushToRemove error prevents updateBatch.Flush from executing.

findings.title: All field assignments missing
findings.message: The Go function assigns GlobalBytes from global.Bytes, NeedBytes from need.Bytes, GlobalItems as the sum of global.Files+global.Directories+global.Symlinks, NeedItems as the sum of need.Files+need.Directories+need.Symlinks, NeedDeletes from need.Deleted, Sequence from the sequence parameter, and RemoteState from the state parameter. The Rust function returns FolderCompletion::default(), so none of these seven fields are populated, producing semantically wrong completion data for every caller.

findings.title: Missing setCompletionPct call
findings.message: The Go function calls comp.setCompletionPct() at line 834 before returning, which computes the completion percentage from GlobalBytes and NeedBytes (typically as (GlobalBytes-NeedBytes)/GlobalBytes * 100). The Rust function never invokes any equivalent computation, so the CompletionPct field will remain at its default value (likely 0.0) rather than reflecting the actual ratio, breaking all downstream consumers that rely on the completion percentage.

findings.title: Rust function accepts no parameters
findings.message: The Go function signature accepts four parameters (global db.Counts, need db.Counts, sequence int64, state remoteFolderState) and uses them to populate every field of FolderCompletion. The Rust function takes zero parameters, making it impossible to replicate the Go behavior; all callers will receive a default-initialized struct with zero/empty values instead of the computed values derived from global and need counts, the sequence number, and the remote folder state.

findings.title: Return type collapses error and not-found
findings.message: Go returns (protocol.FileInfo, bool, error) at lib/model/model.go:114, providing three distinguishable outcomes: (file, true, nil) for found, (zero, false, nil) for not-found, and (zero, false, err) for errors such as folder-not-configured or DB failure. The Rust implementation returns Option<db::FileInfo>, which collapses the not-found case (bool=false, err=nil) and every error case (err!=nil) into a single None. Callers that must distinguish "folder does not exist" from "file not present in folder" — required by scenario/daemon-api-surface and scenario/folder-type-behavior — cannot do so through this signature.

findings.title: Missing GetGlobalFile lookup and receive-only resolution
findings.message: Go lines 641-665 perform a database lookup via GetGlobalFile and execute a four-branch type-switch: propagate DB error, no-op when global not found, call Remove and return (true, nil) when the global item is receive-only-changed and the local item is deleted, and replace the local FileInfo with the global FileInfo when the folder is ReceiveOnly/ReceiveEncrypted and the two are equivalent. The entire block is absent in Rust, meaning deleted receive-only files are never pruned, equivalent local-changed items are never merged back to the global version, and DB errors are silently swallowed.

findings.title: Missing encrypted-parent-directory cleanup
findings.message: Go lines 633-638 check whether the folder is ReceiveEncrypted, the FileInfo is a directory, and the path matches an encrypted parent pattern; if the on-disk directory is empty it is removed and the method returns (false, nil), preventing the item from entering the batch. The Rust implementation has no such guard, so encrypted parent directories will be unconditionally appended to the update batch, corrupting receive-encrypted folder state.

findings.title: Missing return type and error signaling
findings.message: Go Update returns (bool, error) allowing callers to detect skipped files (false) and propagate database errors; Rust returns unit, so every caller unconditionally treats the file as accepted and no error path exists, breaking all callers that branch on the bool or handle the error.

findings.title: Revert logic replaced with scheduling flags
findings.message: The Go `Revert` method calls `f.doInSync(f.revert)`, which executes the full `revert` function inside a synchronization wrapper. The `revert` method (defined elsewhere in `folder_recvonly.go`) performs the actual revert operation: iterating over locally-changed files, restoring them to the global state, and updating the database. The Rust implementation replaces this entire operation with setting two boolean flags (`scanScheduled` and `pullScheduled`), which merely schedules future scan and pull cycles but never performs the core revert logic—deleting local overrides, restoring global versions, and emitting the corresponding database mutations. This is a complete behavioral gap, not a deferred implementation.

findings.title: Missing file queue processing with rename optimization
findings.message: Go lines 437-513 pop files from a priority queue and attempt a rename-from-deletion-candidate optimization using BlocksHash buckets before falling back to handleFile. It also checks file availability across devices (fileAvailability) and disk space (CheckAvailableSpace) before processing. The Rust implementation has no queue, no rename optimization, no availability check, and no space check.

findings.title: Missing ignore and IgnoreDelete semantics
findings.message: Go lines 337-346 skip deleted files when IgnoreDelete is configured and mark pattern-ignored files as invalid via dbUpdateChan. The Rust function performs no ignore matching and no IgnoreDelete check, breaking the conflict-and-ignore-semantics parity test.

findings.title: Return type mismatch loses deletion state
findings.message: Go returns (map[string]protocol.FileInfo, []protocol.FileInfo, error) — a map of pending file deletions and a slice of directory deletions that callers use to perform deletions after file pulls complete. Rust returns usize (a count), discarding all deletion state required by downstream processing.

findings.title: Stub implementation missing all categorization logic
findings.message: The Rust function is a trivial loop that calls handleFile on each path and counts successes. The Go method (lines 326-427) sorts needed files into multiple categories — ignored files, Windows-invalid filenames, deleted directories, deleted symlinks, deleted regular files, regular files needing pull, unsupported symlinks on Windows/Android, directories, and symlinks — and dispatches each to distinct handlers (deleteFile, deleteFileWithCurrent, shortcutFile, handleDir, handleSymlink, queue.Push, dbUpdateChan sends). None of this dispatch logic exists in Rust.

findings.title: Scope changed from all-devices to single-device lookup
findings.message: Go `DeviceStatistics()` takes no arguments and returns statistics for every known device as `map[protocol.DeviceID]stats.DeviceStatistics`. The Rust port adds a `device: &str` parameter and returns data for only that one device. This breaks every caller that expects an aggregate map of all device statistics in a single call and changes the fundamental API contract from "enumerate all" to "point lookup."

findings.title: Error return dropped silently
findings.message: Go signature returns error and short-circuits before emitDiskChangeEvents when updateLocals fails (lib/model/folder.go:1321-1322), but the Rust function returns unit and on DB-persist failure (line 1181-1184) merely logs via newScanError then falls through to emitDiskChangeEvents (line 1190) and forcedRescanPaths removal (lines 1187-1189), so callers cannot detect the failure and disk-change events fire for data that was never persisted to the database.

findings.title: Revert body replaces doInSync(revert) with flag-setting
findings.message: The Go method delegates to doInSync(f.revert), which acquires synchronization, executes the real revert logic (the f.revert method containing file-set reversion, index update, and completion tracking), and then releases synchronization. The Rust implementation instead only sets two boolean scheduling flags (scanScheduled and pullScheduled) without invoking any revert logic or synchronization wrapper, meaning the actual folder reversion (reverting the file set to the global state, sending index updates, etc.) never occurs. This is a complete behavioral mismatch, not a stub or partial implementation, because the flags it sets are unrelated to the revert operation's contract.

findings.title: Missing folder state transitions
findings.message: Go sets state to `FolderScanWaiting` (line 472), then `FolderScanning` (line 510), and defers `FolderIdle` (line 473). Rust performs no state transitions at all, so any component or UI that observes folder state will never see scanning activity and may make incorrect scheduling decisions.

findings.title: Missing ignore-hash-change deferred pull
findings.message: Go lines 453-470 capture the ignore-pattern hash before scanning and, on exit, compare it to the new hash; if changed, it calls `ignoresUpdated()` and `SchedulePull()`. Rust has no equivalent, so ignore-pattern changes during a scan will not trigger a re-pull, causing newly-unignored files to remain un-synced until the next manual trigger.

findings.title: Return type discards errors
findings.message: Go `scanSubdirs` returns `error` to the caller, allowing upstream logic (scheduling, retry) to react to scan failures. Rust returns `()` and silently swallows errors via `newScanError`, making it impossible for callers to distinguish success from failure. This breaks any caller that gates behavior on the scan result.

findings.title: Empty-subdirs early return inverts Go behavior
findings.message: When `subdirs` is empty the Rust function clears `forcedRescanPaths` and returns `Ok(0)`, skipping all processing. In Go (lib/model/folder.go:792), the subdir filter is only applied when `len(subDirs) > 0 && subDirs[0] != ""`; when subdirs is empty or the first element is the empty string, all deleted files are processed without any prefix filter. The Rust behavior therefore suppresses the entire scan for the "full scan" case.

findings.title: Entire method body is unimplemented stub
findings.message: The Rust function performs only a trivial filter of `forcedRescanPaths` by subdirectory coverage and returns a removal count. The Go function (lib/model/folder.go:786-921) drains deleted files from a streaming CFiler, checks each against ignore patterns, processes a multi-branch switch handling four distinct cases (already-ignored, newly-ignored with directory-delayed batching, newly-deleted with conflict version clearing, and deleted-receive-only-changed with global file lookup), issues batch updates/removes to the database, flushes the batch when full, and counts actual DB changes. None of this logic exists in the Rust port—no ignore matching, no SetDeleted/SetIgnored, no batch Update/Remove/FlushIfFull, no conflict version zeroing, no receive-only flag clearing, and no global file lookup.

findings.title: Missing critical parameters and dependencies
findings.message: The Go signature accepts `ctx context.Context`, `batch *scanBatch`, `cfiler *streamingCFiler`, and `alreadyUsedOrExisting map[string]struct{}` (lib/model/folder.go:776). The Rust function takes only `subdirs: &[String]`, so it has no access to a cancellation context, no scan batch for DB writes, no streaming CFiler to drain deleted files from, and no set of already-processed names to skip. Without these, the core scan-phase-2 workflow cannot be performed.

findings.title: Missing updateBatch flush callback
findings.message: The Go implementation at folder.go:587-594 initializes `updateBatch` with a `NewFileInfoBatch` callback that (a) checks folder health via `getHealthErrorWithoutIgnores()`, returning an error and logging if unhealthy, and (b) calls `updateLocalsFromScanning(fs)` to persist scanned file metadata. The Rust implementation uses `..Default::default()` and never sets up any equivalent callback or flush function. This means scanned file updates will never be applied to local state, and mid-scan health checks are entirely absent, breaking core scan semantics.

findings.title: Missing config.Wrapper default-folder base
findings.message: Go calls w.DefaultFolder() to obtain a base FolderConfiguration populated with user-configured defaults (e.g. default rescan interval, default ignore-permissions, versioning settings, etc.) before overwriting selected fields. Rust uses Default::default(), which yields zero/empty values for every field. Any caller relying on wrapper-supplied defaults will get a structurally different FolderConfiguration, breaking scenario/folder-type-behavior and scenario/daemon-api-surface parity.

findings.title: Device parameter ignored — per-device semantics lost
findings.message: The Rust `_device` parameter is explicitly unused (underscore-prefixed) and the method delegates to `self.folderCompletion(folder_id)` which computes folder-level completion only. The Go implementation uses the `device` argument to compute per-device completion for a given folder, which is a fundamentally different semantic: a folder may be 100% synced with device A but only 40% synced with device B. Dropping the device dimension collapses this into a single value and breaks all parity tests that assert per-device completion (scenario/daemon-api-surface, scenario/folder-type-behavior, scenario/global-need-decision).

findings.title: Parameter order swapped vs Go signature
findings.message: Go defines `Completion(device protocol.DeviceID, folder string)` (device first, folder second) but Rust defines `Completion(&self, folder_id: &str, _device: &str)` (folder first, device second). Any caller mapping positional arguments from the Go interface will silently pass the device value as the folder and vice versa, producing wrong completion results or lookup failures.

findings.title: Missing updateLocals call
findings.message: The Go method calls f.updateLocals(fs) at line 1329 to persist the pulled file metadata into the local database and propagates any error from that call. The Rust implementation entirely omits this call, replacing it with a loop that removes entries from forcedRescanPaths (lines 1143-1145), which is unrelated behavior not present in the Go source. This means pulled files are never recorded in the local index, breaking synchronization state.

findings.title: Wrong input type loses file metadata
findings.message: The Go method accepts []protocol.FileInfo (rich structs carrying name, size, version vector, block hashes, permissions, etc.) while the Rust method accepts &[String] (bare path strings). This type mismatch means the Rust side cannot forward full file metadata to updateLocals or emitDiskChangeEvents, making faithful reimplementation of either downstream call impossible without a signature change.

findings.title: Return type mismatch: single Option vs full map
findings.message: Go returns `map[string]interface{}`, a heterogeneous map of every connected device's stats keyed by device identifier string. Rust returns `Option<ConnectionStats>`, a typed single-device result. This loses the ability to enumerate all connections in one call, changes the error representation (missing device yields `None` instead of an absent map key), and replaces the loosely-typed `interface{}` values with a concrete `ConnectionStats` struct, which may omit dynamic keys that the Go side inserts into the map at runtime.

findings.title: Signature arity mismatch: Rust adds device parameter
findings.message: Go `ConnectionStats()` accepts no arguments and returns aggregate stats for all connections as `map[string]interface{}`. The Rust port adds a `device: &str` parameter and performs a single-key lookup via `self.connections.get(device)`, changing the method from a bulk-query over all connections to a single-device lookup. Any caller expecting the full map of all connection statistics will receive only one entry (or None), breaking API parity and every consumer that iterates the returned map.

findings.title: Missing DropFilesNamed database side-effect
findings.message: The Go method calls `b.f.db.DropFilesNamed(b.f.folderID, protocol.LocalDeviceID, b.toRemove)` to persist the removal of file entries in the database before clearing the batch. The Rust method performs no database operation at all; it merely clones the pending removals into a return vector and clears the internal list. Even if the caller is expected to issue the DB call, the atomicity guarantee is broken: Go ties the clear to a successful DB write, while Rust separates them unconditionally.

findings.title: Unconditional clear loses retry-on-error semantics
findings.message: In Go, `b.toRemove` is only reset (`b.toRemove[:0]`) after `DropFilesNamed` succeeds. If the DB call fails, the slice retains its contents so a subsequent flush can retry. The Rust version calls `self.toRemove.clear()` unconditionally with no error path, so if the downstream consumer of the returned `Vec` fails to persist the removals, the data is already lost from the batch and cannot be retried.

findings.title: Missing device parameter
findings.message: The Go interface signature `DismissPendingFolder(device protocol.DeviceID, folder string) error` accepts both a device ID and a folder string, meaning it dismisses a pending folder offer from a specific device. The Rust implementation `DismissPendingFolder(&mut self, folder: &str)` omits the `device` parameter entirely, making it impossible to dismiss per-device pending folder entries and breaking the daemon API surface contract.

findings.title: Wrong data structure operated on
findings.message: The Rust implementation removes from `self.foldersRunning`, which tracks actively running folders, not pending folder offers. The Go method `DismissPendingFolder` is expected to remove entries from the pending-folders observation store (typically `db.ObservedDevice`/`db.PendingFolder` records keyed by device+folder). Operating on the wrong collection means the method neither dismisses the pending offer nor preserves running folder state, causing both a missing side-effect and a destructive unintended side-effect.

findings.title: Error path silently swallowed
findings.message: Go's signature returns a second value `func() error` that lets the caller discover errors encountered during or after iteration (e.g., database I/O failure, corruption). The Rust implementation chains `.ok()` on the RwLock read (line 634) and `.ok()` on the inner `all_global_files` call (line 635), then falls back to `unwrap_or_default()` (line 636), converting every error—poisoned lock, database failure, missing folder—into an empty `Vec`. Callers cannot distinguish "folder has zero global files" from "database is unreachable," breaking error-reporting contracts required by `scenario/crash-recovery` and `scenario/daemon-api-surface`.

findings.title: Database and disk comparison logic missing
findings.message: Go calls scanner.CreateFileInfo to build a FileInfo from the on-disk stat and then invokes IsEquivalentOptional with a six-field FileInfoComparison struct (ModTimeWindow, IgnorePerms, IgnoreBlocks=true, IgnoreFlags=LocalAllFlags, IgnoreOwnership conditioned on fromDelete or SyncOwnership, IgnoreXattrs conditioned on fromDelete or SyncXattrs) to detect local modifications; Rust has none of this logic, meaning local file changes that should trigger a rescan are never detected.

findings.title: Deferred scan-channel send replaced with direct ScheduleScan
findings.message: Go uses a deferred closure that sends the file name into scanChan only when the returned error is errModified, allowing batched or asynchronous scan scheduling keyed by filename; Rust unconditionally calls self.folder.ScheduleScan() with no filename context, losing per-file scan granularity and changing the scheduling mechanism.

findings.title: Signature and return semantics completely different
findings.message: Go method accepts six parameters (name, stat, item, hasItem, fromDelete, scanChan) and returns an error that callers inspect to distinguish errModified from wrapped CreateFileInfo errors from nil; Rust accepts only a pre-computed bool and returns unit, eliminating all caller-side error discrimination and breaking any upstream code that branches on the error kind.

findings.title: hasItem and item.Deleted guard missing
findings.message: Go returns errModified when the item is absent from the database (!hasItem) or is marked Deleted, treating a file that appeared from nowhere as modified; Rust has no equivalent branch, so files with no database entry or flagged as deleted will not be recognized as requiring a scan.

findings.title: Completely different function signature and semantics
findings.message: The Go function takes a single slice of protocol.FileInfo and persists them to the local database via f.db.Update(folderID, LocalDeviceID, fs), then queries the resulting sequence number. The Rust function instead takes two slices (local and remote) of VersionedFile and calls compute_need(local, remote), which is an entirely different operation — it computes a diff/need-set rather than updating local file metadata in the database. The inputs, core logic, and purpose are not equivalent.

findings.title: Missing LocalIndexUpdated event emission
findings.message: The Go function at lines 1354-1360 logs a LocalIndexUpdated event containing the folder ID, item count, filenames, sequence number, and a legacy "version" field equal to sequence. The Rust function emits no event, breaking any downstream consumer (UI, other devices, event subscriptions) that depends on this notification.

findings.title: Missing database persistence
findings.message: The Go function calls f.db.Update(f.folderID, protocol.LocalDeviceID, fs) at line 1337 to persist the updated file records to the database and returns the error if that fails. The Rust function performs no database write at all, meaning local index updates are silently lost.

findings.title: Missing sequence query and error path
findings.message: The Go function at line 1350 calls f.db.GetDeviceSequence to retrieve the current local sequence number after the update, and returns an error if that call fails. The Rust function has no equivalent query and no error return at all (it returns Vec<String> instead of Result), eliminating two distinct error-propagation paths present in the Go code.

findings.title: Missing duration parameter
findings.message: The Go interface declares `DelayScan(folder string, next time.Duration)` with a `next` parameter of type `time.Duration` that controls how long to delay the next scan. The Rust implementation `DelayScan(&mut self, folder_id: &str)` omits this parameter entirely, and the delegated call on line 438 likewise passes no duration. Any caller that needs to specify a non-default delay duration cannot do so, breaking semantic equivalence for all non-trivial uses of this method.

findings.title: Missing Reschedule call
findings.message: The Go function unconditionally calls `f.Reschedule()` at line 1029 after scanning, which resets the periodic scan timer. The Rust function has no corresponding reschedule invocation on any code path, so after the first timer firing, periodic scans will not be re-armed.

findings.title: Missing error return and propagation
findings.message: The Go function signature returns `error` (line 1015) and propagates the result of `scanSubdirs` to its caller, which uses it for health reporting. The Rust function returns `()`, discarding any scan failure. Callers cannot observe or react to scan errors, breaking crash-recovery and health-check semantics.

findings.title: Missing initial-scan-finished signaling
findings.message: The Go implementation checks whether the `initialScanFinished` channel has already been closed via a non-blocking select; if it has not, it logs either the scan error or a success message and then closes the channel, ensuring downstream consumers (e.g., folder-start waiters) are unblocked exactly once. The Rust implementation contains no equivalent flag, atomic, or oneshot-channel close, so the initial-scan-finished event is never signaled, breaking any component that waits on it.

findings.title: Flush condition checks wrong quantity
findings.message: Go checks only `len(b.toRemove) >= maxToRemove` (line 620) to decide whether to flush removes, using a dedicated constant threshold applied solely to the remove queue. Rust checks the combined sum `self.updateBatch.len() + self.toRemove.len() >= threshold`, which conflates the two independent batch sizes into a single comparison. This means a large updateBatch with an empty toRemove can trigger a flush in Rust but never would in Go at this check, and conversely a full toRemove queue paired with a small updateBatch may fail to trigger when it should.

findings.title: Two-stage independent flush collapsed into single all-or-nothing flush
findings.message: Go performs two independent flush decisions: first it conditionally flushes only the remove queue via `flushToRemove()` (line 621), then unconditionally delegates to `updateBatch.FlushIfFull()` (line 625) which applies its own separate threshold to the update batch. Rust replaces this with a single `self.Flush()` that drains both queues atomically or not at all. This loses the Go behavior where the update batch can be flushed independently even when the remove queue is below threshold, and where removes can be flushed while the update batch is retained.

findings.title: Wrong field written
findings.message: Go sets `file.InodeChangeNs` (inode change time / ctime) but Rust sets `fi.modified_ns` (modification time / mtime). These are semantically different filesystem timestamps; writing to `modified_ns` corrupts the modification time and leaves the inode-change-time field unset, breaking any downstream logic that depends on either field (e.g., conflict detection, database comparison).

findings.title: Wrong data structure mutated
findings.message: The Go interface method `DismissPendingDevice` dismisses a device from the pending/observed-devices store (backed by the database layer `db.ObservedDevice` as shown by `PendingDevices` at lib/model/model.go:125), but the Rust implementation calls `self.connections.remove(device)` which removes the device from the active connections map — an entirely different data structure. This means pending devices are never actually dismissed, and legitimate active connections could be erroneously dropped.

findings.title: Missing 3 enum variants
findings.message: Go defines 7 arms (dbUpdateHandleDir, dbUpdateDeleteDir, dbUpdateHandleFile, dbUpdateDeleteFile, dbUpdateShortcutFile, dbUpdateHandleSymlink, dbUpdateInvalidate) but Rust only defines 5 variants (Update, DeleteFile, DeleteDir, Invalidate, Shortcut), completely omitting equivalents for dbUpdateHandleDir, dbUpdateHandleFile, and dbUpdateHandleSymlink. Any code path that would produce or match these three update types is unrepresentable in the Rust port, breaking folder synchronization for directories, regular files, and symlinks.

findings.title: Missing equivalence check and default-branch version reset
findings.message: Go (lines 132-146) has two distinct branches: an `IsEquivalentOptional` check that adopts the global file when local is equivalent, and a `default` branch that resets `fi.Version` to an empty vector when local genuinely differs from global. Rust collapses both into a single else-branch that always adopts the global file. When local and global are not equivalent, Go produces a record with an empty version vector (strictly older, no conflict), while Rust produces a record with the global's version, breaking conflict-resolution semantics and the folder-type-behavior parity test.

findings.title: Missing-global fallthrough instead of skip
findings.message: Go (line 109-113) skips files whose global entry is missing by logging a Failure event and executing `continue`. Rust `Ok(None) => {}` falls through to lines 2061-2063, unconditionally pushing the local file into `updates`. This causes files with no global counterpart to be persisted with their receive-only flag stripped instead of being skipped, corrupting the local index.

findings.title: No disk deletion of local-only files
findings.message: Go (lines 83-87, 122-131) uses a `deleteQueue` backed by `deleteItemOnDisk`/`deleteDirOnDisk` to physically remove locally-created files from the filesystem before marking them deleted in the database. Rust only sets `local.deleted = true` and clears block hashes without any filesystem deletion, leaving orphaned files on disk that will be re-discovered on the next scan, causing an infinite revert-rescan loop.

findings.title: Version vector never reset
findings.message: Go resets `fi.Version = protocol.Vector{}` in three places (lines 119, 131, 146) to ensure reverted or deleted files have a strictly-older version that does not conflict. Rust never resets any version vector, so reverted files retain their original versions, defeating the "strictly older" invariant required for correct re-synchronization after revert.

findings.title: Missing ReceivedFile notification
findings.message: The Go routine calls `f.ReceivedFile(lastFile.Name, lastFile.IsDeleted())` when a handled or deleted file job was seen in the batch (Go lines 1771-1773, guarded by `found`). This notification is absent in Rust, breaking downstream completion tracking.

findings.title: Missing database commit via updateLocalsFromPulling
findings.message: The Go routine calls `f.updateLocalsFromPulling(files)` on every batch flush (Go line 1769), which persists pulled file metadata to the database. The Rust stub never writes anything to the database, so pulled files will not be recorded and will be re-pulled on every restart.

findings.title: Missing directory fsync side-effect
findings.message: The Go routine opens each changed parent directory (or the directory itself for `dbUpdateHandleDir` jobs) and calls `Sync()` on it unless `DisableFsync` is set (Go lines 1752-1764). The Rust stub performs no filesystem sync at all, risking data loss on crash, which directly violates the crash-recovery parity test requirement.

findings.title: Rust function is a no-op stub
findings.message: The Rust implementation is an empty stub that only returns `jobs.len()`. It is missing all core behavior: batching with a 1000-item cap and 2-second flush timer, directory fsync logic (lines 1752-1764 in Go), the call to `updateLocalsFromPulling` (line 1769), the `ReceivedFile` notification for file deletions/modifications (lines 1771-1774), the `updateFileInfoChangeTime` call for non-deleted non-invalid files (lines 1804-1816), and the sequence-number reset to zero (line 1817). The function signature also changes the streaming channel-based design (`<-chan dbUpdateJob` with a select loop) to a synchronous slice parameter (`&[dbUpdateJob]`), eliminating the ability to flush partial batches on the 2-second ticker independent of incoming jobs.

findings.title: Scan side-effect omitted
findings.message: Go calls f.Scan(scanList) at line 1847 to actually schedule the filesystem scan after pulling; the Rust method only returns a Vec<String> without invoking any scan operation, so the core side-effect of this routine is absent unless every call-site independently performs the scan, which is not enforced by the API contract.

findings.title: Missing folder-type branch for hashing vs no-hashing
findings.message: Go lines 710-714 branch on FolderTypeReceiveEncrypted to call scanner.WalkWithoutHashing instead of scanner.Walk, avoiding expensive hash computation for encrypted folders. Rust always calls hash_file_blocks at line 1410 regardless of folder type; there is no concept of WalkWithoutHashing, meaning receive-encrypted folders will be incorrectly hashed and will produce different FileInfo block_hashes values than Go peers, breaking wire compatibility for encrypted folder sync.

findings.title: Missing ignore/matcher pattern integration
findings.message: Go passes f.ignores as scanConfig.Matcher (line 691) so the scanner skips files matching user-configured ignore patterns (.stignore). Rust's scan_filesystem_deterministic does not consult any ignore patterns during the walk or file processing; should_skip_scan_path (line 1340) only checks hardcoded prefixes like .stfolder and .DS_Store. Files that should be ignored per .stignore will be indexed and synced, breaking the conflict-and-ignore-semantics parity test.

findings.title: Missing rename detection in scan loop
findings.message: Go lines 744-756 call findRename for folder types other than ReceiveOnly and ReceiveEncrypted, performing a second batch.Update and incrementing the changes counter when a rename is detected. The Rust scan_filesystem_deterministic performs no rename detection whatsoever during the filesystem walk; the findRename method exists at line 564 but is never invoked during scanning, causing renamed files to be treated as a delete plus an add instead of a single rename operation, which changes both the changes count and the DB update sequence emitted to peers.

findings.title: Return type drops streaming cfiler and already-used map
findings.message: Go returns (int, *streamingCFiler, map[string]struct{}, error) where the streamingCFiler carries collected deleted files for Phase 2 delete processing and the map tracks files already used for rename detection. Rust returns Result<ScanExecutionStats, String> with no cfiler or tracking map. The caller in Go relies on the returned cfiler to process Phase 2 deletions; without it, the Rust call-site must handle delete detection entirely within scan_filesystem_deterministic (lines 1080-1091), but this collapses the two-phase architecture and changes how delete batching interacts with the scan batch flush logic.

findings.title: Entire function body implements wrong logic
findings.message: The Rust function truncates `forcedRescanPaths` to `maxToRemove` entries and returns a count, which has no relation to the Go function's purpose. The Go function performs version cleanup by calling `f.versioner.Clean(ctx)`, managing IO limiter acquisition/release, transitioning through three folder states (FolderCleanWaiting → FolderCleaning → FolderIdle), and resetting the cleanup timer. None of these operations exist in the Rust implementation; the entire body appears to be copied from a different function.

findings.title: Missing IO limiter acquire and release
findings.message: The Go function acquires an IO limiter token via `f.ioLimiter.TakeWithContext(ctx, 1)` before performing work and defers `f.ioLimiter.Give(1)` to release it. This throttles concurrent IO-heavy operations. The Rust function has no limiter interaction, so it cannot participate in IO backpressure and may allow unbounded concurrent IO when other folder operations also expect limiter coordination.

findings.title: Missing state machine transitions
findings.message: The Go function sets state to `FolderCleanWaiting` on entry, transitions to `FolderCleaning` after acquiring the IO limiter, and defers a reset to `FolderIdle` on exit. The Rust function performs no state transitions at all, which breaks any observer or UI that depends on folder state to report cleanup progress.

findings.title: Missing timer reset
findings.message: The Go function resets `f.versionCleanupTimer` with `f.versionCleanupInterval` at line 1049 to schedule the next periodic cleanup. The Rust function does not reset any timer, so periodic version cleanup will fire at most once and never recur.

findings.title: Missing versioner.Clean call
findings.message: The core side effect of the Go function—invoking `f.versioner.Clean(ctx)` to purge old file versions—is entirely absent from the Rust implementation. Without this call, old versioned files will accumulate indefinitely, violating the version-cleanup behavioral contract.

findings.title: Deleted files not filtered out
findings.message: Go skips files that are already deleted (`fi.IsDeleted()` at Go line 63). Rust only checks for the FLAG_LOCAL_RECEIVE_ONLY bit and does not skip deleted files, so already-deleted entries will be redundantly processed and potentially re-persisted with a new sequence number, corrupting sequence ordering expectations.

findings.title: Missing FolderScanning/FolderIdle state transitions
findings.message: Go sets the folder state to FolderScanning before iterating and defers FolderIdle (Go lines 46-47), which is observable by the UI and other subsystems that gate operations on folder state. Rust performs no state transitions, so concurrent operations and UI status reporting will behave incorrectly during a revert.

findings.title: No directory collection or revertHandleDirs call
findings.message: Go separates directories from files, collecting directory names (Go lines 67-70) and passing them to `revertHandleDirs` (Go line 88) for depth-ordered removal. Rust has no directory-specific handling; directories are processed identically to files, meaning directory entries on disk are never removed and the depth-first deletion order required to successfully remove nested directory trees is absent.

findings.title: Side-effect persistence replaced with data extraction
findings.message: In Go, updateBatch.Flush() is itself a persistence call that writes accumulated file updates to the database as a side effect. The Rust implementation replaces this with a std::mem::swap that merely drains the Vec out of the struct and returns it to the caller, deferring all persistence responsibility. The method no longer performs a flush; it performs an extract, fundamentally changing the call-site contract and making correctness dependent on every caller remembering to persist the returned data.

findings.title: Lock-poisoning error silently swallowed
findings.message: The .ok()? on the RwLock read guard converts a lock-poisoning PoisonError into None, making it indistinguishable from a missing file. The Go equivalent would surface this as an error in the error return value, allowing callers and crash-recovery paths to detect and handle the corruption. This silent swallowing violates scenario/crash-recovery expectations.

findings.title: FileInfo replacement with global entry missing
findings.message: Go line 664 reassigns fi = gf so that the item appended to the batch is the global FileInfo (not the local scan result) when the two are equivalent on a ReceiveOnly or ReceiveEncrypted folder. Rust always pushes the original file argument, so the batch will contain locally-scanned metadata instead of the canonical global metadata, causing version-vector and flag divergence on receive-only folders.

findings.title: Missing batch Remove call for deleted receive-only items
findings.message: Go line 649 calls b.Remove(fi.Name) to schedule removal of the file when it is deleted and the global entry is a receive-only-changed item. Rust has no Remove invocation, so stale deleted receive-only entries will persist in the database and never be cleaned up, directly affecting the crash-recovery and conflict-and-ignore-semantics parity tests.

findings.title: Missing doInSync synchronization wrapper
findings.message: The Go code executes the revert through `doInSync`, which acquires the folder's operation lock, runs the provided function, and handles state transitions (e.g., setting folder state to syncing/idle and emitting events). The Rust `Revert` takes `&mut self` but has no equivalent synchronization or state-transition envelope, meaning concurrent callers could observe or mutate inconsistent folder state even if the revert logic were later filled in.

findings.title: Missing context cancellation checks
findings.message: Go checks ctx.Done() at three points: inside the main loop (line 331), after the loop (line 429), and inside the queue loop (line 439), returning ctx.Err() on cancellation. The Rust function has no cancellation mechanism, which means it cannot be interrupted and will not propagate cancellation errors, affecting crash-recovery semantics.

findings.title: Missing error propagation from database lookups
findings.message: Go calls sdb.GetDeviceFile (lines 370, 389) and sdb.GetGlobalFile (line 450) with explicit error returns that abort the entire function on failure. The Rust function signature has no error return path (returns usize), silently swallowing any errors from handleFile via is_ok(), preventing proper crash-recovery behavior.

findings.title: Error return path removed
findings.message: Go's signature returns `(map[…]stats.DeviceStatistics, error)`, allowing callers to distinguish a legitimate empty result from a database or internal failure. The Rust port uses `unwrap_or_default()` and has no `Result` wrapper, silently returning an empty map when the device key is absent or when an underlying error should be surfaced. This violates the error-propagation contract required by parity tests such as `scenario/daemon-api-surface` and `scenario/crash-recovery`.

findings.title: Structured return type flattened to BTreeMap<String, i64>
findings.message: Go returns `map[protocol.DeviceID]stats.DeviceStatistics` where `stats.DeviceStatistics` is a struct with typed fields (e.g., `LastSeen time.Time`, `LastConnectionDurationS float64`). The Rust port returns `BTreeMap<String, i64>`, collapsing all fields into string-keyed integer values. This loses temporal (`time.Time`) and floating-point (`float64`) fidelity, and any downstream consumer expecting the structured type or its JSON wire representation will see schema-incompatible output.

findings.title: DeviceID typed parameter replaced with bare &str
findings.message: Go `ConnectedTo` accepts `protocol.DeviceID`, a strongly-typed `[32]byte` array with defined encoding (base32 + Luhn check groups). The Rust port accepts `&str`, losing all type-level guarantees: callers can pass arbitrary strings, no validation occurs, and correctness depends entirely on the connections map using the exact same string serialization that Go's `DeviceID.String()` produces. If any code path inserts keys using a different format (e.g., raw hex, lowercase, no dashes), the lookup silently returns `false` instead of matching—a behavioral divergence that would be a compile-time error in the Go version.

findings.title: Event type argument missing
findings.message: Go passes two arguments to emitDiskChangeEvents: the file-info slice and events.LocalChangeDetected (lib/model/folder.go:1324), but the Rust call passes only the scanned slice with no event-type discriminator, risking the wrong event type being emitted or the LocalChangeDetected vs RemoteChangeDetected distinction being lost.

findings.title: Events emitted for unfiltered input
findings.message: Rust calls emitDiskChangeEvents with the original unfiltered scanned slice, which includes paths that were skipped by should_skip_scan_path (line 1160) and paths whose build_local_file_snapshot failed (line 1170), whereas Go passes only the FileInfo objects that were successfully provided to updateLocals, so the Rust version can emit spurious change events for items never persisted.

findings.title: Input type contract changed
findings.message: Go accepts []protocol.FileInfo (pre-built structured file metadata) and delegates directly to updateLocals, whereas Rust accepts &[String] (raw paths) and internally constructs file snapshots via build_local_file_snapshot with manual sequence numbering (lines 1150-1171), shifting metadata-construction responsibility into this function and changing the caller contract.

findings.title: Missing doInSync synchronization wrapper
findings.message: The Go Revert method wraps its work inside doInSync, which serializes folder operations (typically acquiring a mutex, checking folder state, deferring state restoration, and running the callback). The Rust version takes only &mut self with no equivalent serialization guard, so concurrent callers could observe or mutate inconsistent folder state if this method is ever invoked outside an already-held exclusive lock, violating the synchronization contract of the Go original.

findings.title: Missing ScanCompleted call
findings.message: Go calls `f.ScanCompleted()` (line 570) on the success path, which typically updates sequence numbers and notifies watchers. Rust never calls any equivalent, so downstream components waiting on scan completion will not be notified.

findings.title: Missing batch flush between phases
findings.message: Go creates a `scanBatch` (line 513) and explicitly flushes it after Phase 1 (line 540) and after Phase 2 (line 565), ensuring database writes from changed-and-new detection are persisted before deleted-and-ignored detection runs. Rust has no visible batch or flush, risking Phase 2 operating on stale data or accumulated writes being lost on early return.

findings.title: Missing empty-subDirs normalization between phases
findings.message: Go lines 545-549 check after Phase 1 whether `subDirs` is empty and, if so, reset it to `[""]` so Phase 2 traverses the entire folder tree. Rust passes the same `normalized` slice to both phases unchanged, so a full-folder scan may cause Phase 2 to skip traversal entirely if `normalized` is empty.

findings.title: Phase 2 missing cfiler and alreadyUsedOrExisting
findings.message: Go Phase 1 (line 531) returns a `cfiler` and `alreadyUsedOrExisting` set that are passed into Phase 2 (line 556) to coordinate which files have already been processed and to provide current-file information. Rust Phase 2 receives neither, so it cannot distinguish already-processed files from truly deleted ones, risking false deletion detection.

findings.title: Return value semantic mismatch
findings.message: The Rust function returns a count of entries removed from `forcedRescanPaths`, while the Go function (lib/model/folder.go:948) returns the number of actual database changes (batch updates and removes) made during deleted/ignored file processing. Callers relying on the change count to decide whether to commit or announce changes will receive a meaningless value from the Rust port.

findings.title: Subdirectory prefix matching semantics differ
findings.message: The Rust code requires an exact match (`pending == sub`) or a `/`-delimited prefix (`pending.starts_with(&format!("{sub}/"))`). The Go code at lib/model/folder.go:795 uses `strings.HasPrefix(fi.Name, sub)` with no separator requirement, so a subdirectory `"foo"` in Go matches file `"foobar"` while in Rust it does not. This changes which files are included in the scan scope.

findings.title: scanErrors field conflated with errorsMut mutex
findings.message: In Go, `errorsMut` is a `sync.Mutex` protecting the `scanErrors` slice, and errors are appended to `f.scanErrors` (lib/model/folder.go:1268). In Rust, errors are pushed directly onto `self.errorsMut` (line 667), treating the mutex name as the error list. This means the Rust struct has no separate `scanErrors` collection; any code reading `scanErrors` elsewhere will not find accumulated errors, and the data model diverges from Go's two-field (mutex + slice) design.

findings.title: Folder reference downgraded to bare string ID
findings.message: Go stores a full folder reference (`f: f` at folder.go:584) in the `scanBatch`, giving the batch's callback access to methods like `getHealthErrorWithoutIgnores()` and `updateLocalsFromScanning()`. Rust stores only `self.config.id.clone()`, a plain string. Even if the missing callback were later added, the batch would lack the object reference needed to invoke health checks or persist scan results, requiring an architectural change to restore parity.

findings.title: Missing fsType parameter
findings.message: Go accepts an fsType config.FilesystemType parameter and assigns it to fcfg.FilesystemType (lib/model/model.go:3460). The Rust signature omits this parameter, so FilesystemType always takes its Default value. If Default does not match the caller-intended filesystem type (e.g. FilesystemTypeFake for tests), the folder will operate on the wrong filesystem abstraction, affecting scenario/folder-type-behavior and scenario/crash-recovery.

findings.title: Missing label parameter
findings.message: Go accepts a label string parameter and assigns it to fcfg.Label (lib/model/model.go:3459). The Rust signature omits the label parameter entirely, so the resulting FolderConfiguration.label is always an empty string. Any caller that passes a non-empty label will silently lose it, producing wire-incompatible folder metadata.

findings.title: Missing error return path
findings.message: Go returns `(FolderCompletion, error)` allowing callers to distinguish between a valid zero-completion and an error (e.g., unknown folder, database failure). Rust returns only `FolderCompletion` with no error channel, so error conditions such as a nonexistent folder will either panic or silently return a default/zero completion, which is observably different behavior on the wire (the REST API surfaces this error to clients).

findings.title: Error propagation removed
findings.message: The Go method returns error and propagates failures from updateLocals (lines 1329-1331). The Rust method returns unit and silently discards the Result from emitDiskChangeEvents via let _ = (line 1146). Callers in Go can detect and react to persistence failures; Rust callers cannot, which may cause silent data-loss scenarios tested by scenario/crash-recovery.

findings.title: Missing event-type discriminator
findings.message: The Go call at line 1332 passes events.RemoteChangeDetected as the second argument to emitDiskChangeEvents, distinguishing remote-pull events from other disk-change sources. The Rust call passes only the pulled slice with no event-type parameter, so emitted events will either default to the wrong type or be indistinguishable from locally-detected changes, breaking consumers that filter on event type.

findings.title: Visibility restricts public interface method to crate-internal
findings.message: Go declares `ConnectionStats` on the exported `Model` interface, making it part of the public API surface. The Rust port marks it `pub(crate)`, preventing external crates (including the daemon API layer and any test harness outside the crate) from calling it, which will break the `scenario/daemon-api-surface` parity test.

findings.title: Return type mismatch breaks error contract
findings.message: Go returns `error` (nil on success, non-nil on DB failure). Rust returns `Vec<String>`, providing no mechanism to signal failure. Callers in Go can propagate the error upward (e.g., aborting a scan); Rust callers receive a value that is always considered successful, eliminating the error-handling branch that exists in every Go call-site of `flushToRemove`.

findings.title: Missing error return
findings.message: The Go interface returns `error`, allowing callers to detect and handle failures (e.g., database errors when removing the pending record). The Rust function returns unit `()`, silently discarding any failure. This breaks callers that rely on error propagation, including the REST API layer which maps this error to HTTP status codes.

findings.title: Lazy iterator replaced with eager Vec
findings.message: Go returns `iter.Seq[db.FileMetadata]`, a lazy pull-based iterator that streams results one at a time without materializing the full result set in memory. The Rust port returns `Vec<db::FileMetadata>`, which eagerly collects every record into a heap-allocated vector before the caller sees any element. For folders with millions of global entries this changes the memory profile from O(1) streaming to O(n) resident, likely violating the `memory-cap-50mb` parity test and altering backpressure semantics for any caller that short-circuits iteration early.

findings.title: Missing error-closure return value changes API contract
findings.message: Go's `AllGlobalFiles` returns two values `(iter.Seq[db.FileMetadata], func() error)`, where the second is a deferred error reporter typically called after iteration completes. The Rust port returns only `Vec<db::FileMetadata>` with no error channel. Any caller that relied on the error closure to commit, rollback, or log post-iteration status has no equivalent mechanism, breaking the interface contract and any upstream code that pattern-matches on the two-return-value shape.

findings.title: CreateFileInfo error path absent
findings.message: Go wraps and returns errors from scanner.CreateFileInfo (e.g., permission errors reading xattrs) without sending to scanChan, distinguishing transient I/O failures from actual modifications; Rust has no error path, so I/O failures during stat comparison are silently ignored rather than propagated.

findings.title: fromDelete parameter and conditional ownership/xattr ignore missing
findings.message: Go conditions IgnoreOwnership and IgnoreXattrs on the fromDelete flag combined with folder-level SyncOwnership/SyncXattrs settings, meaning delete operations skip ownership and xattr checks; Rust has no fromDelete parameter and no comparison configuration, so delete-triggered scans cannot relax these checks as the Go code requires.

findings.title: Vec-to-Set data-structure change drops duplicates
findings.message: Go appends to a slice (`append(b.toRemove, item)`), preserving duplicates; Rust inserts into a set (`self.toRemove.insert(…)`), which silently deduplicates. If `Remove` is called twice with the same path, Go's `toRemove` contains two entries and downstream iteration processes the path twice, whereas Rust's contains one. Any consumer that relies on duplicate presence (e.g., counting removals, or idempotency guards that expect to see every call reflected) will behave differently.

findings.title: Return type incompatibility
findings.message: The Go function returns error (nil on success). The Rust function returns Vec<String> containing computed need paths. No caller of the Go function expects a list of needed paths back; this return value has no equivalent in the original API contract and suggests the Rust function was mapped from a different Go method.

findings.title: Wrong items removed from forcedRescanPaths
findings.message: The Go function iterates over the input FileInfo slice and removes each file.Name from forcedRescanPaths (lines 1343-1347), i.e., it removes the names of files that were just updated locally. The Rust function instead removes need.need_paths — the output of a need computation between local and remote — which is a different set of paths. This changes which files are eligible for forced rescan.

findings.title: Return type divergence changes error contract
findings.message: The Go interface method returns nothing (`DelayScan(folder string, next time.Duration)` — no error return), meaning callers cannot observe whether the folder exists via this call's return value. The Rust version returns `Result<(), String>` and explicitly errors with `ErrFolderMissing` on line 437 when the folder is absent. This introduces an error path that does not exist in the Go contract; Go callers and API surfaces that rely on DelayScan being fire-and-forget will behave differently if the Rust side propagates or logs this error, which affects the daemon-api-surface and crash-recovery parity scenarios.

findings.title: Error-path collapse loses paused/not-running distinction
findings.message: Go's checkFolderRunningRLocked (lib/model/model.go:3243-3256) returns three distinct sentinel errors depending on folder state: ErrFolderMissing ("no such folder") when the folder config does not exist, ErrFolderPaused ("folder is paused") when the config exists but is paused, or ErrFolderNotRunning ("folder is not running") when the config exists and is not paused but the runner is absent. The Rust FolderErrors implementation only checks folderRunners.get(folder_id) and returns ErrFolderMissing for any absent key, collapsing all three error cases into one. API consumers that branch on error type (e.g., lib/api/api.go:903 checks errors.Is(err, ErrFolderPaused)) will receive incorrect errors and take wrong code paths.

findings.title: Pull errors from tempPullErrors never merged into Errors output
findings.message: Go's folder.Errors() (lib/model/folder.go:1298-1301) merges both scanErrors and pullErrors into a single returned slice. In Rust, the folder struct has only errorsMut (folder_core.rs:363) with no separate pullErrors field. The sendReceiveFolder struct accumulates pull errors in tempPullErrors (folder_core.rs:1648) during copierRoutine, but these are never transferred into folder.errorsMut. Since folder::Errors() (folder_core.rs:483-484) only returns errorsMut.clone(), pull errors routed through tempPullErrors are silently dropped from the FolderErrors response, causing data loss visible to the REST API and event system.

findings.title: Returned errors not sorted by path
findings.message: Go's folder.Errors() (lib/model/folder.go:1302-1304) sorts the merged error slice by Path using slices.SortFunc with strings.Compare, producing deterministic alphabetical ordering in the REST API response. Rust's folder::Errors() returns self.errorsMut.clone() with no sorting, so the API response order is insertion-order-dependent and will differ from Go's output, breaking scenario/daemon-api-surface parity.

findings.title: Missing context/cancellation parameter
findings.message: The Go function accepts a `context.Context` (line 1015) and passes it to `scanSubdirs` and the logger, enabling cooperative cancellation during long scans. The Rust function takes only `&mut self`, so there is no mechanism for the caller to cancel an in-progress scan, which is required for clean shutdown and folder-pause semantics tested by scenario/crash-recovery.

findings.title: Spurious forced-rescan branching not present in Go
findings.message: The Rust implementation introduces a branch on `forcedRescanPaths.is_empty()` that dispatches to either `Scan` or `handleForcedRescans`. The Go `scanTimerFired` has no such branching; it unconditionally calls `scanSubdirs(ctx, nil)`. Forced-rescan handling in Go is managed elsewhere (e.g., in the folder loop or `SchedulePull`). This changes the method's contract and may cause forced rescans to be consumed at the wrong point in the lifecycle.

findings.title: Hardcoded constant replaced with caller-supplied parameter
findings.message: Go uses the package-level constant `maxToRemove` for the remove-queue threshold and `updateBatch.FlushIfFull()` uses its own internal constant. Rust exposes `threshold` as a caller-supplied `usize` parameter, shifting responsibility for providing the correct value to every call site and removing the guarantee that the canonical constant is used consistently.

findings.title: Missing Lstat and error path
findings.message: Go performs `f.mtimefs.Lstat(file.Name)` internally to obtain the current on-disk inode change time and propagates the Lstat error to the caller. Rust accepts a pre-resolved `ts_ns` parameter and has no error return (`()` instead of `Result`). Unless every call-site in the Rust port replicates the Lstat call, error check, and early-return-on-error before invoking this function, the error-propagation contract of Go is lost and callers may proceed with stale or invalid timestamps on I/O failure.

findings.title: Missing zero-time guard
findings.message: Go checks `!ct.IsZero()` and explicitly sets `InodeChangeNs = 0` when the platform does not support inode change time (zero value). Rust unconditionally assigns `ts_ns` with no zero-check, so a zero or negative sentinel value from the caller will be stored verbatim rather than being normalized, potentially producing different behavior on platforms that do not report ctime (e.g., some Windows or Plan 9 filesystems).

findings.title: Device parameter type mismatch
findings.message: The Go method accepts `protocol.DeviceID`, a fixed-length 32-byte cryptographic identifier with specific marshaling, comparison, and `String()` formatting semantics. The Rust function accepts `&str`, losing type safety, length validation, and canonical formatting. Any caller passing a non-canonical or malformed string will silently operate on the wrong key rather than failing at the type level.

findings.title: Missing error return
findings.message: The Go signature returns `error` (`DismissPendingDevice(device protocol.DeviceID) error`), but the Rust function returns unit `()`. Callers cannot distinguish success from failure (e.g., device not found, database I/O error), breaking the daemon-api-surface parity where HTTP handlers inspect and propagate this error to respond with appropriate status codes.

findings.title: Different delete method called
findings.message: Go line 113 calls `f.deleteDirOnDisk(dir, scanChan)`, passing the scan channel into the delete operation so that intermediate scan entries can be emitted during deletion. Rust calls `self.sendReceiveFolder.deleteDirOnDiskHandleChildren(&dir)`, a differently-named method that receives no scan/rescan channel. The "HandleChildren" suffix implies different child-entry semantics, and the absence of a scan channel parameter means any in-deletion scan notifications produced by the Go path are lost in Rust.

findings.title: Missing error reporting on delete failure
findings.message: Go line 114 calls `f.newScanError(dir, fmt.Errorf("deleting unexpected dir: %w", err))` when `deleteDirOnDisk` returns an error, making the failure observable to the scan-error subsystem. The Rust code at line 2148 checks `.is_ok()` only to increment a counter and silently discards the error, so failed directory deletions are never recorded as scan errors. This loses error observability required by crash-recovery and folder-type-behavior scenarios.

findings.title: All string representations differ from Go
findings.message: Every string returned by the Rust method diverges from the Go original: Go returns prefixed names like "dbUpdateDeleteDir", "dbUpdateDeleteFile", "dbUpdateShortcutFile", "dbUpdateHandleInvalidate" while Rust returns shortened forms "delete_dir", "delete_file", "shortcut", "invalidate". If these strings are logged, compared, serialized, or used in any wire/schema context, the mismatch will cause silent incompatibility or matching failures. The Rust variant "Update" maps to "update" which has no single Go counterpart at all.

findings.title: Already-deleted file not distinguished in receive-only-global branch
findings.message: Go (line 118-121) checks `if fi.Deleted` before attempting disk deletion when the global is receive-only-changed; if already deleted, it only resets the version vector and breaks to append. Rust unconditionally sets `local.deleted = true` and clears block hashes regardless of prior deletion state, potentially re-deleting or corrupting metadata for files that were already locally deleted.

findings.title: No FolderScanning/FolderIdle state transitions
findings.message: Go (lines 76-77) sets the folder state to `FolderScanning` on entry and defers `FolderIdle` on exit. Rust performs no state transitions, so external observers (UI, event subscribers, other goroutines) will not see the folder enter scanning state during revert, breaking status reporting and potentially allowing concurrent operations that Go would serialize.

findings.title: No directory deletion flush phase
findings.message: Go (lines 157-171) flushes queued directory deletes after the main loop, creating synthetic FileInfo entries (Type=Directory, Deleted=true, Version=empty, ModifiedBy=shortID, ModifiedS=now) for each successfully removed directory. Rust has no equivalent post-loop phase, so locally-created directories are never recorded as deleted in the database and are never removed from disk.

findings.title: SetDeleted semantics not replicated
findings.message: Go (line 130) calls `fi.SetDeleted(f.shortID)` which sets `ModifiedBy` to the local short ID, sets the `Deleted` flag, clears blocks, and updates modification time. Rust only sets `local.deleted = true` and clears `block_hashes`, omitting `ModifiedBy` assignment, which causes the deleted record to lack provenance and may break cluster-wide delete propagation.

findings.title: Missing sequence reset to zero
findings.message: The Go routine explicitly sets `job.file.Sequence = 0` (Go line 1817) before appending to the batch, ensuring the database layer assigns a new sequence number. The Rust stub does not reset sequences, which would cause stale or duplicate sequence numbers if the rest of the pipeline were implemented.

findings.title: Missing updateFileInfoChangeTime call
findings.message: For non-deleted, non-invalid files the Go routine calls `f.updateFileInfoChangeTime(&job.file)` (Go lines 1809-1815) to capture the on-disk inode change time. This is absent in Rust, meaning subsequent scans would see a stale change time and unnecessarily re-process files.

findings.title: DB and lock errors silently swallowed
findings.message: `self.sdb.read().ok()?` (line 571) discards a poisoned-lock error, and `.ok()` on `get_global_file` (line 573) discards any database-layer error, both converting them to `None`. In Go, these paths surface a non-nil `error` to the caller. This means the Rust port will silently succeed (return "not found") in scenarios where the Go code propagates actionable errors, violating the crash-recovery and daemon-api-surface parity scenarios.

findings.title: Return type loses error channel
findings.message: Go returns `(protocol.FileInfo, bool, error)` — three distinct signals: the file data, a found-boolean, and an error. Rust returns `Option<db::FileInfo>`, collapsing "not found" (`false, nil` in Go) and "error occurred" (`false, err` in Go) into the same `None` variant. Callers that need to distinguish a missing file from a database or folder-lookup error (e.g. the daemon API surface returning 404 vs 500) cannot do so, breaking parity with every call-site that inspects the error return.

findings.title: Channel aggregation absent
findings.message: Go's pullScannerRoutine blocks on a channel (line 1837) and aggregates all paths sent during the entire pull phase until the channel is closed; the Rust version performs no channel consumption or temporal aggregation—it snapshots self.queue at a single point in time, so paths arriving after the call but before pull completion are silently lost.

findings.title: Deduplication missing
findings.message: Go inserts every received path into a map[string]struct{} (line 1835-1838) to deduplicate before scanning; Rust collects self.queue verbatim via iter().cloned().collect(). If self.queue is a Vec or any ordered collection that admits duplicates, the same path can appear multiple times in the returned list, causing redundant scan work and divergent behavior from Go.

findings.title: Missing context cancellation and channel drain on flush error
findings.message: Go lines 727-733 on FlushIfFull error explicitly cancel the scan context and drain the channel before returning, preventing goroutine leaks and ensuring the scanner walk terminates cleanly. Rust's flush_scan_updates_if_full at line 1053 on error sets scan_error but the walk continues processing paths until the closure checks scan_error at line 1034; there is no mechanism to interrupt walk_deterministic mid-traversal, so the walk completes fully before the error is observed, wasting resources and potentially producing side effects on an aborted scan.

findings.title: Per-file errors halt entire scan instead of continuing
findings.message: Go lines 722-725 handle per-file scan errors by calling newScanError and continuing to the next file, allowing the scan to complete with partial errors recorded. Rust's process_scanned_file calls fs::symlink_metadata at line 1387 and returns Err on failure, which propagates through process_path (line 1050) to set scan_error, halting the entire scan. This means a single inaccessible file aborts the scan of the entire folder, which differs from Go behavior and will fail crash-recovery scenarios where partial results are expected.

findings.title: Wrong signature: return type and missing context
findings.message: The Go function signature is `func (f *folder) versionCleanupTimerFired(ctx context.Context)` returning nothing, while the Rust function is `fn versionCleanupTimerFired(&mut self) -> usize` returning a count and accepting no cancellation context. The return type mismatch will propagate to callers, and the missing context means the function cannot support cooperative cancellation on the IO limiter acquire path.

findings.title: Wrong scan dispatch mechanism
findings.message: The Rust port calls ScheduleForceRescan(&[]) instead of the Go-equivalent DelayScan(0). In Go (folder.go:266-268), DelayScan(0) sends a zero duration into the scanDelay channel, which resets the scan timer to fire immediately for a normal scan. The Rust ScheduleForceRescan (folder_core.rs:515-525) instead inserts an empty-string root entry into self.forcedRescanPaths and calls forcedRescanRequested(), which sets scanScheduled=true. This contaminates the forcedRescanPaths set with a persistent root-level entry that was never intended by the Go code, and the Rust DelayScan method (folder_core.rs:477-478) will early-return if forcedRescanPaths is non-empty, meaning a subsequent normal DelayScan will be silently suppressed. The Go code never modifies forced rescan paths in scanOnWatchErr; it only resets the scan timer.

findings.title: SetDeleted semantics differ — missing shortID
findings.message: Go calls `fi.SetDeleted(f.shortID)` (Go line 76) which stamps the file's modified-by field with the local device short ID. Rust sets `local.deleted = true` without recording a modifier identity, which may cause downstream version-vector or conflict-resolution logic to behave differently when comparing deletion origins.

findings.title: Execution order reversed
findings.message: Go calls flushToRemove first (line 613), then updateBatch.Flush (line 616). Rust drains updateBatch first (line 280), then calls flushToRemove (line 281). If flushToRemove mutates state that updateBatch.Flush depends on (or vice versa), the reversed order produces different observable behavior. The Go ordering also ensures removes are committed before updates, which may be semantically significant for conflict resolution.

findings.title: Hardcoded "local" device string instead of typed local device ID
findings.message: The Rust implementation passes the string literal "local" as the device argument to get_device_file. In Go, CurrentFolderFile resolves the local device via protocol.LocalDeviceID, a typed [32]byte constant. If the Rust DB layer does not consistently map the string "local" to the same canonical local-device identity used during index insertion, lookups will silently return None for files that exist, causing silent data loss in scenario/global-need-decision and scenario/index-sequence-behavior.

findings.title: Visibility narrowed from public to pub(crate)
findings.message: The Go interface method CurrentFolderFile is exported (uppercase) and is part of the public Model interface at lib/model/model.go:114, making it callable by any package including the REST API layer. The Rust function is declared pub(crate), restricting access to the crate only. If the REST/API layer or any external consumer resides in a separate crate, it will not be able to call this method, breaking scenario/daemon-api-surface parity.

findings.title: Missing Windows/Android platform-specific branches
findings.message: Go lines 348-359 and 403-410 handle Windows-invalid filenames and unsupported symlinks on Windows/Android by either recording pull errors or marking files as unsupported. These platform branches are entirely absent in Rust, which would cause behavioral divergence on those platforms.

findings.title: DeviceID key type downgraded to plain String
findings.message: Go uses `protocol.DeviceID` (a fixed-length byte-array type with Luhn check-digit validation and canonical string encoding) as both the outer map key and the `device` parameter type. The Rust port accepts `&str` and keys its return map on `String`, removing all format validation and allowing malformed device IDs to be silently accepted, which can cause wire-level mismatches with the Go daemon.

findings.title: Visibility downgraded from public interface to pub(crate)
findings.message: Go `ConnectedTo` is declared on the exported `Model` interface (lib/model/model.go:123), making it part of the public API surface consumed by REST handlers, the GUI backend, and other packages. The Rust method is marked `pub(crate)`, restricting it to crate-internal use only. Any external consumer that relied on this method through the `Model` trait (e.g., the daemon API surface tested by `scenario/daemon-api-surface`) would fail to compile, breaking API parity.

findings.title: Extra forcedRescanPaths removal
findings.message: The Rust version unconditionally removes every scanned path from forcedRescanPaths, but the Go snippet contains no such logic in updateLocalsFromScanning; if this behavior was relocated from another call site it should be verified, otherwise it adds side effects absent from the Go function.

findings.title: Missing IO limiter acquisition
findings.message: Go acquires an IO limiter permit with context cancellation (lines 475-478), bounding concurrent disk IO across folders. Rust has no rate-limiting, allowing unbounded concurrent scans that may saturate disk IO.

findings.title: Missing clearScanErrors before scan
findings.message: Go line 511 calls `f.clearScanErrors(subDirs)` to reset per-file scan errors for the directories about to be scanned. Rust has no equivalent, so stale scan errors from previous runs will persist and may mislead users or other components.

findings.title: Missing setError(nil) on healthy start
findings.message: Go line 459 calls `f.setError(ctx, nil)` after a successful health check to clear any previously recorded folder error. Rust does not clear prior errors, so a folder that recovers from a transient problem will continue to appear errored.

findings.title: Missing warning log side effect
findings.message: The Go method emits a structured warning log `f.sl.Warn("Failed to scan", slogutil.FilePath(path), slogutil.Error(err))` on every call (lib/model/folder.go:1267). The Rust implementation performs no logging at all. This removes an observable side effect that operators rely on for diagnosing scan failures and that parity tests under scenario/crash-recovery and scenario/folder-type-behavior may assert on.

findings.title: No concurrency guard around error list mutation
findings.message: The Go method explicitly acquires `f.errorsMut.Lock()` before mutating and releases it after (lib/model/folder.go:1266,1272), protecting concurrent access from multiple goroutines. The Rust method takes `&mut self`, which provides compile-time single-owner exclusivity but does not enforce runtime mutual exclusion if the struct is wrapped in `Arc<RwLock<…>>` or similar shared-ownership container. If callers share this struct across async tasks or threads (as the Go code implies), the locking semantics must be enforced at the call site or via an interior-mutex wrapper; neither is visible here.

findings.title: toRemove not pre-allocated to maxToRemove capacity
findings.message: Go explicitly pre-allocates `toRemove` with `make([]string, 0, maxToRemove)` at folder.go:585, reserving capacity to avoid repeated allocations during scanning. The Rust side relies on `Default::default()`, which typically yields a zero-capacity `Vec`. While not a correctness bug, this diverges from the Go behavior under memory-cap-50mb test scenarios where allocation patterns matter, and could cause excess reallocations during large scans.

findings.title: Device type downgraded from DeviceID to plain string
findings.message: Go uses `protocol.DeviceID` (a fixed-length byte array with validation and canonical formatting) while Rust accepts `&str`. This loses compile-time type safety and allows malformed device identifiers to reach the function without validation, which can cause divergent behavior when the API receives an invalid device ID (Go would reject at the type boundary; Rust would pass it through).

findings.title: Spurious forcedRescanPaths removal
findings.message: The Rust implementation adds a loop removing each pulled path from self.forcedRescanPaths. No corresponding logic exists in the Go updateLocalsFromPulling method (lines 1328-1334). This injected behavior may prematurely clear rescan requests that should persist until a separate code path clears them, potentially affecting scenario/folder-type-behavior tests.

findings.title: Empty-input guard omitted
findings.message: Go explicitly checks `len(b.toRemove) > 0` before performing any work, avoiding unnecessary DB calls on an empty batch. The Rust version always allocates and returns a `Vec` and calls `clear()` regardless. While functionally benign in isolation, this diverges from Go's guard pattern and would cause a spurious DB call if the DB interaction were restored, and it introduces a needless allocation of an empty `Vec` on every no-op flush.

findings.title: Insertion-order vs set-order iteration divergence
findings.message: Go's slice guarantees iteration in insertion order. Rust's `HashSet` yields an arbitrary iteration order (or `BTreeSet` yields lexicographic order).
