If any downstream consumer of `toRemove` is order-sensitive—such as logging, batched database operations, or conflict resolution that depends on first-seen ordering—the port will produce different results under identical inputs.

findings.title: ErrFolderMissing string drift
findings.message: Go defines ErrFolderMissing as "no such folder" (lib/model/model.go:195) while Rust defines it as "folder missing" (model_core.rs:19). Any client or test that matches on this error string for API compatibility or error-type discrimination will see a different value, breaking wire-level behavioral equivalence.

findings.title: Scan invocation argument mismatch
findings.message: Go passes `nil` to `scanSubdirs` (line 1016), semantically meaning "scan everything with no subdirectory filter." Rust passes `&[]` (an empty slice) to `Scan`. If `Scan` treats an empty slice differently from a None/absent value (e.g., scanning nothing vs. scanning everything), this is a behavioral divergence. Verify that `Scan(&[])` is semantically equivalent to `scanSubdirs(ctx, nil)`.

findings.title: Update batch unconditional flush-check path missing
findings.message: In Go, `b.updateBatch.FlushIfFull()` on line 625 is always called regardless of whether the remove queue was flushed, meaning the update batch is checked and potentially flushed on every invocation. In Rust, when the combined-size condition on line 289 is false, `None` is returned and no flush check of the update batch occurs at all, so a full update batch will never be flushed until the combined total also exceeds the threshold.

findings.title: Concurrency model lost around scan processing
findings.message: Go lines 105-107 spawn a concurrent `pullScannerRoutine` goroutine consuming a channel, and each directory is sent to that channel (line 116) so scan processing overlaps with subsequent deletions. Rust replaces this with a synchronous `ScheduleForceRescan` call per directory with no concurrent consumer. If `pullScannerRoutine` performs I/O or batching that depends on receiving items while deletions are still in flight, the Rust port will exhibit different timing and possibly different scan-batch boundaries, affecting crash-recovery correctness.

findings.title: Return type changed from void to usize
findings.message: Go declares `revertHandleDirs` with no return value (line 100). Rust returns `usize` representing a removed-directory count. This changes the method contract; callers in Go never inspect a return value, so any Rust caller relying on the count has no Go-equivalent behavior, and the count itself only reflects successful deletes rather than total attempts, which could mislead upstream logic.

findings.title: Panic on unknown value not replicated
findings.message: Go panics with a formatted message on any unrecognized dbUpdateType value (line 100), providing a fail-fast diagnostic. Rust uses a closed enum so exhaustive matching prevents this at compile time for known variants, but if the enum is ever extended or received from an external source (e.g., deserialization), the lack of a catch-all error path diverges from Go's defensive behavior. This is lower severity only because Rust's type system partially compensates.

findings.title: No pullScannerRoutine or scan channel
findings.message: Go (lines 79-81) creates a scan channel and launches `pullScannerRoutine` in a goroutine so that deleted paths are rescanned during revert. Rust has no equivalent, so filesystem changes made during revert are not incrementally scanned, requiring a full scan afterward to reconcile the filesystem with the database.

findings.title: Unbounded Vec violates memory cap
findings.message: Go uses `FileInfoBatch` with `FlushIfFull()` (lines 149-150) to bound memory during revert by flushing to the database in chunks. Rust collects all updates into an unbounded `Vec<FileInfo>` (line 2038), which for large folders may exceed the 50 MB memory cap required by the `memory-cap-50mb` parity test.

findings.title: Signature eliminates streaming and timer-based flush
findings.message: The Go function accepts a channel (`<-chan dbUpdateJob`) and uses a `select` loop with a 2-second ticker to flush partial batches under low throughput (Go lines 1748-1749, 1823-1824). The Rust function accepts a synchronous slice (`&[dbUpdateJob]`), making it impossible to implement the timer-based partial-flush behavior without a redesign of the calling contract.

findings.title: Visibility narrower than Go interface
findings.message: The function is declared `pub(crate)`, restricting it to the current crate. The Go `Model` interface exports `CurrentGlobalFile` as a fully public method available to any importing package. If external crates (e.g. an API or REST handler crate) need to call this method — as the Go REST layer does — they will be unable to, breaking the daemon-api-surface scenario.

findings.title: Empty-set guard missing
findings.message: Go checks len(toBeScanned) != 0 (line 1841) and skips the scan entirely when no paths were received; Rust unconditionally returns whatever self.queue contains, including an empty Vec, which may lead callers to invoke a no-op scan if they do not replicate this guard.

findings.title: Queue not drained after collection
findings.message: In Go, the map is function-local and the channel is fully consumed, so no residual state remains after the routine returns. The Rust method reads self.queue but does not clear or drain it, meaning subsequent calls would return stale/duplicate entries unless the caller explicitly clears the queue, risking double-scanning.

findings.title: Missing scanner config fields for permissions, xattrs, normalization, and progress
findings.message: Go scanner.Config (lines 688-706) includes IgnorePerms, AutoNormalize, Hashers count, ShortID, ProgressTickIntervalS, LocalFlags, ModTimeWindow, EventLogger, ScanOwnership, ScanXattrs, XattrFilter, and TempLifetime. None of these have equivalents in the Rust implementation. While some may be deferred features, LocalFlags (line 700) and ModTimeWindow (line 701) directly affect whether a file is considered changed, meaning the Rust scan may produce different update decisions than Go for the same filesystem state.

findings.title: Missing shutdown guard on scan trigger
findings.message: Go's DelayScan(0) (folder.go:267-270) uses a select statement that races the send on f.scanDelay against f.done, silently discarding the scan request if the folder is shutting down. The Rust port unconditionally mutates state (forcedRescanPaths and scanScheduled) with no shutdown check, which could schedule a scan on a folder that is mid-teardown, potentially causing post-shutdown scan execution or state corruption.

findings.title: Error parameter type narrows from error interface to bare string
findings.message: The Go parameter `err error` is an interface; the method calls `err.Error()` to extract the string (lib/model/folder.go:1269). The Rust parameter is already `&str`, so the caller must pre-convert. This shifts responsibility and loses the ability to inspect the original error type inside `newScanError`, which could matter if future logic branches on error kind or wraps the error.

findings.title: Debug logging absent
findings.message: Go emits a per-path debug log "Scheduling scan after pulling" via slog.DebugContext (line 1844) for observability during pull operations; the Rust implementation has no logging, reducing diagnostic parity in production troubleshooting.
